comments.text: Quanyu Tang and I have just uploaded our paper Consecutive integers free of certain prime factors to the arXiv. This paper is a human-written version of the AI-generated paper that Quanyu shared here in the comment section two months ago. With $\theta = \frac{21}{40}$ we prove that for all large enough $k$ and all $n$ with $$2k < n \le \exp\left(\frac{\log^2 k}{20 \log \log k}\right), \qquad (1)$$there must be a prime $p \in (k, k + 3k^{\theta})$ that divides the product $P := (n-k)\cdots(n-1)$. In particular, we have the lower bound $$n_k > \exp\left(\frac{\log^2 k}{20 \log \log k}\right) \qquad (2)$$for all large enough $k$, showing that $n_k$ indeed grows faster than any polynomial. A corresponding Lean formalization can be found here, which, apart from a result by Baker-Harman-Pintz stating that the number of primes in $I := (k, k + k^{\theta})$ is $\gg \frac{k^{\theta}}{\log k}$, is completely self-contained. For a very high-level overview of the proof: it is based on a theorem by Konyagin from his paper Estimates of the least prime factor of a binomial coefficient in which he gives the best known lower bound for #1095. Given a smooth function $f(x)$, Konyagin's theorem gives a beautifully general upper bound on the number of integers $m$ for which $f(m)$ is close to a rational number with small denominator. The crux (i.e. the reason that such a theorem is relevant) is that if the product $P$ is not divisible by a prime $p \in I$, then $n \bmod{p}$ must be close to $p$, so that the fraction $\frac{n}{p}$ must be close to an integer. Applying Konyagin's upper bound to the function $f(x) := \frac{n}{x}$ (for an $n$ for which $(1)$ above holds) and combining it with a lower bound on the number of primes in $I$, shows that there must be a prime $p \in I$ for which $f(p)$ is not close to an integer, which implies that $p$ divides $P$. ChatGPT of course deserves credit for $(2)$, although I must admit that I have barely looked at the write-up it produced. Once it was realized that this problem is quite similar to #1095 (and I suggest that these problems should link to one another), and that Konyagin's ideas can also be applied here, that was sufficient to start writing the paper Quanyu and I just uploaded. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
Congrats and thanks for the subsequent work which makes us confident in the result!

After iterating GPT-5.5 Pro more than a dozen times, it produced a note claiming a good lower bound for \(n_k\). More precisely, the draft claims to prove that there exists an absolute constant \(c>0\) such that, for all sufficiently large \(k\),\[n_k>\exp\left(c\frac{(\log k)^2}{\log\log k}\right).\]In particular, this would imply Erdős's conjecture that $n_k>k^d$ for all constant \(d\). The draft has passed several AI-based checks, but I do not have time to manually verify every detail of the proof carefully. Therefore I am posting it here, and I would be very grateful for any comments, corrections, or independent checks. The PDF is available here: pdfhere. The tex source is available here: texhere.
Congrats! Standard check found no issues.
A386620 is now published (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Here is a heuristic proof of this Problem. Let\[ \mathcal P_k=\{p:\ p\ \text{prime},\ k<p<2k\},\qquad M_k=\prod_{p\in\mathcal P_k}p. \]The condition that the whole block $[n-k,n-1]$ contains no multiple of any $p\in\mathcal P_k$ is equivalent to saying that the $k$ integers $n-1,\dots,n-k$ are all coprime to $M_k$. For a fixed $p\in\mathcal P_k$, the interval $[n-k,n-1]$ contains a multiple of $p$ iff\[ n\equiv 1,2,\dots,k\pmod p. \]Thus the allowed residues mod $p$ are exactly the $p-k$ classes $\{0,k+1,\dots,p-1\}$. By the Chinese Remainder Theorem, the number of allowed residue classes mod $M_k$ is\[ A_k=\prod_{p\in\mathcal P_k}(p-k), \]so the exact density of admissible $n$ is\[ D_k \ :=\ \frac{A_k}{M_k} \ =\ \prod_{\substack{k<p<2k\\ p\ \mathrm{prime}}} \left(1-\frac{k}{p}\right). \]Using summation over primes and the prime number theorem one obtains the asymptotic\[ \log\frac{1}{D_k}=(\log 4+o(1))\,\frac{k}{\log k}. \]Numerically (for $1\le k\le 50$), $\log n_k$ grows on the $\frac{k}{\log k}$ scale and closely tracks $(\log 4)\frac{k}{\log k}$; in this range we in fact observe\[ \log n_k \ \le\ (\log 4)\,\frac{k}{\log k}, \]while we cannot replace $\log 4$ by $\log 3$. Heuristically one expects $n_k\asymp D_k^{-1}$; making this rigorous appears to be the remaining difficulty. Details of the proofs and numerical evidence can be found on my GitHub page here.
